sections.title: Purpose & Scope
sections.content:
This procedure establishes requirements for the correct classification, segregation, storage, and disposal of clinical and infectious waste generated at FIFO mine site health clinics. Improper waste management poses risks to clinic staff, site workers, transport and disposal contractors, and the environment.

This procedure applies to all clinical staff and relevant site personnel involved in waste handling. In Western Australia, clinical waste management is governed by the Health (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1911 (WA), the Environmental Protection Act 1986 (WA), and associated regulations and guidelines. Compliance with these obligations is mandatory.

sections.title: Waste Classification and Segregation
sections.content:
Waste must be segregated at the point of generation into the correct stream. Never mix waste streams.

Clinical and infectious waste (yellow biohazard bag):
- Blood, body fluid-soaked dressings, swabs, and bandages
- Used PPE contaminated with blood or body fluids
- Pathological waste
- Microbiological cultures or specimens
- All waste from a patient with a confirmed transmissible infection

Sharps waste (rigid yellow sharps container: AS/NZS 4031):
- All used needles, syringes, lancets, cannulae, blades
- Never place sharps in yellow biohazard bags; this is a critical safety rule

Pharmaceutical waste (separate labelled container):
- Expired, damaged, or unwanted medications
- Cytotoxic drugs require separate purple-lidded containers
- Never dispose of medications in general or clinical waste streams

General waste (black bag):
- Non-contaminated packaging, paper, gloves without blood or body fluid contamination, empty IV bags without blood

If in doubt about the correct stream, treat the waste as clinical waste (yellow bag): it is safer to over-classify than under-classify.

sections.title: Storage Requirements
sections.content:
Clinical waste must be stored safely while awaiting collection:

- Store in a designated, secure clinical waste storage area; clearly labelled with biohazard signage and inaccessible to unauthorised personnel
- Keep separate from general waste storage areas and away from food preparation, eating, or drinking areas
- Yellow biohazard bags must be sealed and labelled with the site name and date before storage
- Maintain a cool, covered area to minimise decomposition and odour; clinical waste should not be stored in direct sunlight or extreme heat
- Maximum storage periods before collection: generally not more than 30 days at room temperature; check with your approved waste contractor for specific requirements applicable to your site
- Containers must be intact, not overfilled, and protected from vermin, birds, or weather damage
- Maintain a waste storage register listing bag/container sealing date and volume

sections.title: Disposal
sections.content:
Disposal of clinical waste must be through an approved clinical waste contractor licensed under Western Australian environmental legislation.

- Obtain a quote and service agreement from an approved contractor before commencing clinical operations at a new site
- A waste transport certificate or consignment note (manifest) must accompany each collection; retain copies for a minimum of 3 years
- Never place clinical waste in site general waste skips, tip trucks, or arrange for informal disposal
- Never transport clinical waste in a personal vehicle
- If waste contractor access is disrupted (e.g., due to road closures or site access issues), notify the medical director and site HSE manager immediately and arrange secure interim storage
- Pharmaceutical waste must be disposed of through a Pharmaceutical Return program or approved pharmaceutical waste contractor; not clinical waste contractors unless specifically licensed for pharmaceutical waste

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Never place sharps inside yellow biohazard bags; this is a critical rule that protects waste handlers from needlestick injury
- Health (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1911 (WA) and associated regulations mandate compliant clinical waste management; non-compliance can result in significant penalties
- Waste segregation is the responsibility of the person generating the waste; do not leave it for cleaning or ancillary staff to sort
- In remote locations, waste contractor visits may be infrequent; plan storage capacity accordingly and maintain a buffer
- Staff must not enter the clinical waste storage area without appropriate PPE
- Report any accidental contact with clinical waste, waste bin spills, or bag failures via the incident reporting system

sections.title: Documentation
sections.content:
Retain the following records:

- Waste storage register: date sealed, type, approximate volume, date collected
- Waste transport manifests/consignment notes: retain for a minimum of 3 years
- Contractor licence details and service agreements: on file at the clinic
- Incident reports for any waste management breaches or exposures
- Annual review of waste volumes and contractor performance should be documented and forwarded to the medical director
